identity.title: Automotive Oil Change and Lubrication Shops

lens.included_activities: U.S. lower-middle-market quick-lube and preventive-maintenance operators whose primary work is motor-oil changes and chassis lubrication for passenger vehicles, vans, and trucks, including closely associated filter, fluid, battery, bulb, and wiper services delivered by stand-alone company-owned or franchised operating businesses.
lens.excluded_activities: General mechanical repair, tire-only service, dealer and fleet captive maintenance, gasoline stations where oil service is incidental, lubricant manufacturing and distribution, motorcycle service, do-it-yourself retail sales, pure franchisors without operating-company exposure, shells, non-control vehicles, and owner-dependent microshops.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Retail drivers and commercial fleets pay per completed preventive-maintenance service, often in a drive-through or stay-in-vehicle format. Revenue combines a core oil-change ticket with recommended filters, fluids, wipers, batteries, and checks; throughput depends on bay flow, technician teams, inventory availability, trust, and repeat maintenance intervals.

a.bridge: BLS broad NAICS 811190 employment includes management, sales, office support, technicians, and a very large automotive-service-attendant component. Valvoline's exact operating model uses proprietary software to combine vehicle service history, OEM recommendations, and visual technician inspection in roughly 15-minute stay-in-vehicle service. I map customer recognition, service-history review, recommendations, scripted explanation, promotion targeting, inventory forecasting, staffing, training support, and routine administration to AI exposure, while leaving vehicle positioning, draining and filling fluids, filter replacement, physical inspection, safety verification, and equipment maintenance unexposed. The BLS proxy also contains car washes and other services, likely overstating attendant labor for quick lube, while the large-chain source may overstate digitization relative to independent firms.
a.rationale: Standardized customer and recommendation workflows create a useful AI layer, but short cycle time and hands-on service keep the not-yet-automated exposed share below one third in the base case.
a.change_evidence: Exact-industry time studies separating customer interaction, digital recommendation, inspection, under-hood and pit work, inventory, management, and rework would materially change exposure.
caveats: The five-digit occupational source mixes oil-change and car-wash labor models.
AI recommendations still require technician observation and accountable customer approval.

rho.rationale: Quick-lube work is standardized, high-frequency, and already supported by point-of-sale, service-history, OEM-schedule, and multi-site operating systems, which favors five-year implementation of customer, recommendation, forecasting, scheduling, and training tools. Independent ownership, franchise variation, legacy integration, technician turnover, data quality, customer trust, and liability review limit full deployment.
rho.change_evidence: Network and independent-shop adoption cohorts showing active AI-feature use, override frequency, training time, integration cost, and realized cycle-time effects would materially revise implementation.
caveats: Large-chain capabilities are not representative of all target firms.
Short service cycles leave little tolerance for slow or inaccurate recommendations.

e.rationale: The exact code is a coherent, repeat outsourced maintenance service, so most stand-alone operating firms fit the lens. The estimate removes captive dealer and fleet shops, incidental gasoline-station service, pure franchisors, micro owner-dependent locations, and firms outside the target earnings range. Franchise structures and the margin-bridged dataset count make the firm boundary uncertain.
e.change_evidence: Firm-level ownership, franchise status, store count, revenue mix, EBITDA, captive versus external work, and enterprise-to-establishment links would materially change eligibility.
caveats: A franchise location is not necessarily a distinct transferable firm.
The supplied target count is an estimate based on a broad service margin rather than observed exact-industry EBITDA.

s5.bridge: Gallup reports that 22% of U.S. employer-business owners intended to sell, transfer, or go public within five years. I reduce that intention rate for noncompletion, family or employee transfers, franchise transfers that do not convey the targeted operating control, and firms outside the lens. The broad survey can overstate completed external sales, while active consolidation and refranchising in quick lube can make qualifying transfers more frequent than the haircut suggests.
s5.rationale: A meaningful but minority share of eligible operators should undergo a qualifying external control transfer over five years.
s5.change_evidence: Quick-lube closed transactions, franchise resales, broker listings, owner age, and the share of internal versus external transfers would materially change this value.
caveats: The owner survey is broad and measures plans, not closings.
Asset sales, refranchising, and equity control transfers are not interchangeable.

q.rationale: Operators can retain benefit from stronger repeat marketing, improved recommendation consistency, better labor and inventory planning, fewer missed items, and higher bay throughput. Franchise fees, software cost, consumer price competition, technician wage pressure, discounting, customer resistance to upselling, rework, and the physical throughput ceiling absorb or pass through part of the gain.
q.change_evidence: Matched-shop evidence on car count, ticket mix, cycle time, labor hours, recommendation acceptance, repeat visits, pricing, rework, and technology cost would materially change retention.
caveats: Recommendation lift can reflect more services sold rather than lower cost at constant quality.
Aggressive automated upselling can erode trust and retention.

d5.bridge: FHWA projects light-duty VMT growing 0.6% annually through 2043, a roughly 3% five-year tailwind to maintenance exposure. I offset that with expanding electric-vehicle adoption, longer manufacturer oil intervals, and some dealer or do-it-yourself substitution; DOE reports plug-in, all-electric, and fuel-cell vehicles were 7% of model-year 2023 light-duty production, indicating a growing but still minority new-vehicle share. VMT may overstate oil-service quantity when service intervals lengthen, while an aging internal-combustion fleet, commercial usage, and expanded preventive-maintenance tickets can support stronger shop volume.
d5.rationale: The installed combustion-engine fleet should keep oil service durable over five years, but electrification and longer intervals offset modest growth in vehicle travel.
d5.change_evidence: Exact-industry oil-change counts, average maintenance intervals, ICE and hybrid vehicle stock by age, EV penetration, fleet mix, and same-store car counts would materially change the demand ratio.
caveats: New-vehicle technology shares are not the in-service fleet mix.
Oil-change demand depends on mileage, interval policy, and owner compliance rather than VMT alone.

o.rationale: Almost all surviving year-five quantity requires an accountable operator to verify the vehicle and fluid specification, position it safely, remove and replace filters and fluids, inspect for problems, prevent spills, document work, and handle used oil. Do-it-yourself maintenance, dealer capture, longer-life fluids, and limited robotic equipment can remove some independent-shop quantity, but AI alone does not perform the physical service.
o.change_evidence: Evidence on robotic oil-change deployment, do-it-yourself share, dealer and fleet capture, mobile service, and unattended preventive-maintenance formats would materially change operator need.
caveats: Demand lost to EVs belongs primarily in d5, not in the operator-need estimate for remaining oil-service quantity.
Software can reduce labor per visit while an accountable operating business remains necessary.

narrative.executive_view: Quick lube is a coherent repeat service with highly standardized information, recommendation, and operating workflows, but the core job remains physical. AI can improve customer retention, recommendation consistency, inventory, staffing, and training while technicians continue to execute and verify the service.
narrative.ai_impact: AI can recognize returning customers, interpret service history and OEM schedules, draft recommendations, personalize reminders, forecast parts and fluids, support training, schedule labor, and summarize exceptions. Technicians remain necessary for visual inspection, vehicle positioning, under-hood and pit work, fluid and filter handling, final verification, and spill or fault response.
narrative.value_capture: High visit frequency and repeat records support conversion and planning benefits. Competitive pricing, franchise economics, software fees, customer skepticism about upselling, wage pressure, and finite bay throughput prevent full retention.
narrative.firm_availability: The exact code fits the external-service lens, but franchise ownership and multi-site structures make firm counts and control boundaries ambiguous. Captive shops, pure franchisors, micro operators, and businesses outside the earnings band reduce the eligible population.
narrative.demand_durability: A large installed combustion-engine fleet and modest VMT growth preserve the core need over five years. EV adoption, longer oil intervals, do-it-yourself service, and dealer competition keep the base case near flat rather than tracking VMT mechanically.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: The largest uncertainties are the mixed occupational proxy, pace of EV fleet penetration, oil-change interval behavior, franchise transfer structure, independent adoption of integrated tools, and conversion of broad owner intentions into qualifying control sales.
narrative.principal_driver: Standardized vehicle-history, recommendation, retention, inventory, and labor workflows make AI implementation practical across repeated visits.
narrative.principal_weakness: Electrification and longer service intervals pressure underlying quantity while most on-site work remains physical and safety-accountable.

sources.title: 2022 NAICS Definition: 811191 Automotive Oil Change and Lubrication Shops
sources.supports: Official activity definition and cross-reference boundaries used for the lens and operator-need reasoning.

sources.title: May 2023 National Industry-Specific Occupational Employment and Wage Estimates: NAICS 811190
sources.supports: Five-digit other automotive repair and maintenance occupational mix used as a broad task-exposure proxy.

sources.title: Valvoline Inc. 2025 Annual Report on Form 10-K
sources.supports: Primary-company evidence on the 15-minute stay-in-vehicle format, technician inspection, service-history and OEM recommendation software, store network, and franchise structure.

sources.title: 2025 FHWA Forecasts of Vehicle Miles Traveled
sources.supports: Light-duty VMT forecast used as the primary maintenance-demand exposure proxy.

sources.title: Seventy-three Percent of All Light-Duty Vehicles Produced in 2023 Came with Gasoline Direct Injection
sources.supports: DOE vehicle-technology production mix used to bound the near-term electrification headwind.

sources.title: 2022 Economic Census: Other Services Basic Statistics
sources.supports: Exact-industry establishment and revenue context used to qualify firm availability.

sources.title: Most Small-Business Owners Lack a Succession Plan
sources.supports: Employer-owner five-year sale and transfer intentions used as the broad starting point for the qualifying-control-transfer proxy.
